g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Resource Management and Operating System Structures. Let's explore how OS manages resources.
An Operating System is the crucial software that acts as an intermediary between users and computer hardware.
g.text: The OS is the most crucial software running on a computer.
It acts as an intermediary between user and hardware.
Primary purpose: Execute programs conveniently and efficiently.

g.data-narration: The OS must manage two fundamental entities: hardware resources and software resources. This is the core OS function.
The OS is a resource manager. Its key responsibility is to allocate and de-allocate resources fairly and efficiently.
Process Management handles CPU time. The OS decides which process gets the CPU, when, and for how long using scheduling algorithms.
g.text: Process Scheduling: Decides which process gets CPU, when, for how long
Scheduling Algorithms: FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, Priority Scheduling
Process Synchronization: Coordinates execution to avoid conflicts
Deadlock Handling: Manages indefinite waiting situations

g.data-narration: Memory Management ensures efficient use of RAM through allocation, protection, and techniques like paging and segmentation.
g.text: Allocation: Provides memory space to processes when they run
Protection: Isolates memory space of one process from another
Efficiency: Uses paging and segmentation for optimal RAM utilization
Virtual Memory: Uses disk as extension when RAM is full

g.data-narration: File System Management handles storage. It creates/deletes files, schedules disk operations, and controls access to data.
g.text: File Operations: Creation, deletion, and directory management
Disk Scheduling: Optimizes read/write order (SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN)
Access Control: Determines who can read, write, or execute files
Provides uniform logical view of information storage

g.data-narration: Device Management controls all I/O devices through drivers. It provides a uniform interface and manages multiple process requests.
g.text: Device Drivers: Controls all Input/Output devices
Uniform Interface: Provides read(), write() system calls
Request Management: Handles multiple process requests efficiently
Uses buffering and caching for performance optimization

g.data-narration: These four managers work together: Process, Memory, File System, and Device managers form the core OS resource management.
Now lets explore OS Structures - how the operating system is internally organized to perform these management tasks.
Simple/Monolithic: Entire OS written as single large program. Efficient but hard to maintain and debug. Example: Early UNIX.
g.text: Entire OS (kernel) as single large program
Collection of procedures that can call any other
Efficient (low overhead) but Hard to maintain and debug

g.data-narration: Layered Approach: OS broken into layers built upon each other. Simplifies design but has performance overhead due to layering.
g.text: Each layer uses functions from layer below it
Easy design, implementation, debugging but Performance overhead

g.data-narration: Microkernel moves non-essential components to user-space. Kernel only provides IPC, scheduling, memory management. Example: QNX, MINIX 3.
g.text: Non-essential components (file systems, drivers) in user-space
Kernel provides: IPC, basic scheduling, memory management only

g.data-narration: Most modern OS use Modular Kernel: core services plus dynamically loaded modules. Combines performance with modularity. Example: Linux, macOS.
g.text: Dynamically loads modules (drivers, filesystems) as needed

g.data-narration: Heres a comparison table of all four OS structures showing their advantages, disadvantages, and examples.
User programs interact with OS through System Calls - the programming interface to OS services. Lets see how this works.
Example: printf() makes a write() system call. OS checks permissions, buffers data, schedules I/O - all resource managers work together.
g.text: When a C program uses printf(), it makes a write() system call:
User Program - write() - OS Kernel - Device

g.data-narration: During this call: OS checks permissions, buffers data in kernel memory, schedules I/O operation. All resource managers work together.
g.text: 1. OS checks if process has permission to write (Device Management)
2. Buffers data in kernel memory (Memory Management)
3. Schedules I/O operation to display device (Device Management)

g.data-narration: Lets summarize the key points from this lecture on Resource Management and OS Structures.
Primary OS function is Resource Management - fairly and efficiently sharing CPU, Memory, Storage, and I/O among competing processes.
g.text: Primary function of OS is Resource Management
Four main managers: Process, Memory, File System, Device Managers
Ensures fair and efficient allocation of CPU, Memory, Storage, I/O

g.data-narration: OS Structures define internal organization. Modern OS use modular kernel balancing performance, maintainability, and security.
g.text: Four structures: Monolithic, Layered, Microkernel, Modular
System Calls provide programming interface to OS services
Modern OS (Linux, macOS) use Modular Kernel

g.data-narration: To recap: OS manages hardware/software resources through four managers, organized by various structures, accessed via system calls.
g.text: Resource Management = Core OS Function - Four Managers
OS Structures - Accessed via System Calls

g.data-narration: Thank you for watching! This knowledge is fundamental to understanding how operating systems work.
